repairBuyOrReplace.h2: XJ 3.2 V8 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Not repairable — requires full engine replacement with steel-liner unit
Essential maintenance — upgrade to metal tensioners
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Worth it on a well-maintained XJ

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XJ 3.2 V8? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2000 model with steel-liner engine (Nikasil issue resolved)
* Timing chain tensioners upgraded to metal units
* Full service history with regular oil changes
* No cold-start rattling (timing chain tensioner health)
* No coolant loss or overheating history (cooling system health)
avoidIf: * Pre-2000 model with original Nikasil engine — assume it's a liability
* Cold-start rattling and no timing chain history — budget for tensioner work
* No service history — the AJ26 is sensitive to oil quality
* Coolant loss or overheating history — cooling system damage may have occurred
* Excessive rust — X308s are known for corrosion, particularly around wheel arches, sills and front spring mounts

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The XJ 3.2 V8 is Jaguar's entry-level V8 in the X308 generation — a 3.2-litre AJ26 engine producing 240 bhp and offering a superbly quiet and refined ride. The engine is a significant departure from the straight-six and V12 engines it replaced. However, it has two critical vulnerabilities: Nikasil cylinder liners on pre-2000 cars (which can fail catastrophically) and plastic timing chain tensioners (which degrade and can cause engine destruction). Post-2000 cars with steel-liner engines and upgraded metal tensioners are the only safe choices. With typical X308 values ranging from £3,000 to £8,000, an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well-maintained post-2000 example with documented service history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare XJ 3.2 V8 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XJ 3.2 V8 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Engine Type — Confirm post-2000 with steel-liner engine (Nikasil issue resolved) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss may indicate cooling system leaks
* Coolant Leaks — Inspect around coolant reservoir and thermostat housing for cracks or residue
* Rust — Check wheel arches, sills and front spring mounts for corrosion
* Electrical Systems — Test all electrical functions; PDUs and wiring can be problematic
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, cooling or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures and corrosion advisories

items.question: How much does a Jaguar XJ 3.2 V8 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned XJ 3.2 V8 engine typically costs £3,500–£5,500 supply only. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. The cost of a low-mileage Jaguar XJ replacement engine in the UK typically ranges from £3,000 to £6,000. Customers have reported dealer quotes of around £15,000–£30,000 for engine replacement, while reconditioning or rebuilding is often in the £8,000–£10,000 range. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your XJ.

items.question: Is the Jaguar XJ 3.2 V8 engine reliable?
items.answer: The AJ26 is a refined and smooth V8, but it has two critical vulnerabilities. Pre-2000 engines suffer from Nikasil cylinder liner degradation — the nickel-based coating can become porous, leading to compression loss and catastrophic failure. Jaguar addressed this from 2000 onwards with steel-liner engines. Plastic timing chain tensioners are another known weakness — they degrade over time and can cause chain failure. Post-2000 cars with steel-liner engines and upgraded metal tensioners are the only safe choices.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ 3.2 V8?
items.answer: The most common issues are Nikasil cylinder liner failure on pre-2000 cars (catastrophic engine failure), plastic timing chain tensioner failure (rattling on cold start — can destroy the engine), cooling system leaks from plastic components (coolant reservoir, thermostat housing), electrical gremlins (particularly the Power Distribution Unit), and rust (wheel arches, sills and front spring mounts).

items.question: Is it worth replacing an XJ 3.2 V8 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept post-2000 XJ worth £4,000–£8,000, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 50–100% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired pre-2000 car with significant rust or a Nikasil engine, the same recon may exceed our 60% Jaguar threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Post-2000 cars with steel-liner engines command a premium.

items.question: How do I find my Jaguar XJ 3.2 V8 engine code?
items.answer: The engine code is AJ26, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 3.2-litre V8 fitted to the XJ X308 from 1997–2002. The engine produces 240–243 bhp. The engine date code can be found on the horizontal ledge to the right of the warm water thermostat tower.

items.question: Can I repair the timing chain tensioner, or does the engine need replacement?
items.answer: The tensioner and guides must be replaced if worn — upgrading to metal tensioners is essential. A preventative replacement costs £800–£2,000. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £3,500–£6,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.
